hook: Family-run Japanese cafe and matcha bar across from the museums
facts: Open since 2009 on Thurloe Place, near the V&A and Natural History Museum
Serves donburi and katsu alongside an award-listed selection of green teas
Signature matcha lattes, soft serve and parfaits built on ceremonial-grade powder
Now part of a small group with a Soho outpost
w: Across from the museum crowds, a family have whisked ceremonial-grade matcha here since 2009. Rice bowls and katsu fill the lunch trade, but regulars come for the green stuff: lattes, soft serve, parfaits stacked with red bean and white chocolate. South Ken's antidote to the chain coffee march.

hook: Provenance-obsessed rare-tea shop with a postbox in the wall
facts: Tucked on Dering Street just off New Bond Street since 2008
First tea company in the world to put each maker's name and location on every tea
Champions 'small tea' from growers farming under 15 acres across Asia
A red postbox inside lets you mail a tea card directly from the shop
w: Tim d'Offay's tiny Dering Street shop turned tea provenance into a creed: every leaf carries its maker's name and farm. He coined 'small tea' for growers working under fifteen acres, then built a little red postbox into the wall so you can mail a tea card from the counter. Matcha you can trace to the field.

hook: Kyoto matcha house pouring soft serve down a Soho side street
facts: London outpost of a Uji tea house tracing its roots to 1860
Sits on Rupert Street, off the Chinatown edge of Soho
Matcha graded by intensity, from mild lattes to a near-bitter house pour
Soft serve, parfaits and warabimochi made with stone-ground Uji matcha
w: The London arm of a Uji tea house founded in 1860, when matcha meant Kyoto and little else. On a narrow stretch of Rupert Street it grinds its own, then ranks it by strength so you can climb from gentle latte to a pour that turns properly bitter. Soft serve and warabimochi for the unconverted.

hook: Takeaway tea counter inside Japan's cultural embassy on Ken High St
facts: Ground-floor drinks stand just inside Japan House on Kensington High Street
Pours hot and iced matcha, hojicha and sencha from specialist suppliers
Japan House run by the Japanese government as a cultural showcase
Building also holds a gallery, shop and the basement Akira restaurant
w: Just inside Japan House, the Japanese government's polished cultural outpost on Kensington High Street, a small stand pours hot and iced matcha, roasted hojicha and sencha to take away. No queue theatre, no branding circus, just specialist leaf done plainly. Upstairs: a gallery and a shop of things you didn't know you wanted.

hook: Loose-leaf specialist on Neal Street since the early Eighties
facts: Trading from 15 Neal Street as a loose-leaf specialist since 1982
Stocks Japanese green teas including matcha alongside global blends
Closed briefly after a founder's death, then reopened and kept going
Became a sister brand to Reads Coffee Roasters of Dorset in 2024
w: A loose-leaf survivor on Neal Street, selling proper tea since 1982 when the street still smelled of fruit and veg. Walls of caddies, matcha and sencha among the blends, novelty teapots shaped like cats. It nearly vanished after a founder died, then quietly reopened. Covent Garden's last true tea shop.

hook: Tea and ceramics gallery running chanoyu workshops in Notting Hill
facts: Tea and Japanese ceramics gallery at 120 Talbot Road, opened summer 2025
Keiko Uchida has practised the tea ceremony for over 25 years
Runs chanoyu workshops teaching the history and ritual of whisking matcha
Sells high-grade matcha and green tea beside studio pottery
w: Keiko Uchida has whisked matcha the formal way for over twenty-five years, and her Talbot Road gallery makes a quiet shrine of it: shelves of single-estate tea beside studio ceramics chosen by hand. Book a chanoyu workshop and you learn the choreography, kettle to bowl. Ritual, not a takeaway cup.

hook: Natural wine bar that grew out of a Shoreditch shipping container
facts: Began in 2014 in a shipping container at Boxpark Shoreditch
Opened its Dalston Lane bar, shop and garden in 2016
Strong focus on Austrian growers and central European low-intervention wine
Doubles as bottle shop, by-the-glass bar and tasting space
w: It started in a shipping container at Boxpark in 2014, an Austrian leading with the wines his home country never gets credit for. The Dalston Lane bar that followed keeps the bias: Gruner and skin-contact oddities, a back garden, shelves you drink from at shop price. Central European natural wine, championed before it was cool.

hook: Neighbourhood natural wine shop and bar at the Bakers Arms
facts: On Hoe Street near the Bakers Arms, between Walthamstow and Leyton
Stocks several hundred biodynamic and low-intervention bottles
Drink any bottle in for a modest corkage fee
Walled back garden with benches under the trees
w: A shop that became a bar by popular demand, down by the Bakers Arms. Hundreds of biodynamic bottles line the walls; pay the corkage and drink one where you stand, or carry it out to the walled garden under the trees. Georgia, the Jura, Kent. Walthamstow's cellar door, run by people who actually taste the stock.

hook: South London survivor of the bar that lit London's natural wine fuse
facts: Opened on Lordship Lane in 2017 from the team behind the original Terroirs
The Covent Garden original (from 2008) helped launch UK natural wine
Wood tables, exposed brick and a bottle-lined shop wall
French-leaning small plates beside a low-intervention list
w: When the original Terroirs opened off Charing Cross in 2008, London barely knew what natural wine was; it became the bar that taught the city. The Covent Garden room has since closed, but this Lordship Lane offshoot carries the torch: bare wood, brick, a shop wall of bottles, and proper French plates to soak them up.

hook: Cleveland Street wine bar built by two old Terroirs hands
facts: Opened on Cleveland Street near Fitzroy Square in 2013
Founded by David Clawson and Renato Catgiu, who met working at Terroirs
Mostly natural, mostly European list of small low-intervention growers
Italian-leaning small plates change with the wine
w: Two Terroirs alumni broke away in 2013 to open this narrow room at the top of Cleveland Street, near Fitzroy Square. The brief stayed simple: mostly natural, mostly European, mostly small growers, with Italian small plates that bend toward whatever's open. A grown-up bolthole where the list rewards trusting the staff.

hook: Old off-licence reborn as one of London's sharpest wine merchants
facts: At 71 Highbury Park, a short walk from the Emirates
Grew from a traditional off-licence into a serious merchant
Stocks close to 1,400 wines, leaning to small producers
Named International Wine Challenge Retailer of the Year 2025
w: Once a corner offie of the lottery-and-lager school, now one of the capital's most decorated merchants. Around 1,400 bottles crowd 71 Highbury Park, weighted to small growers you won't meet in a supermarket. Tastings, a tucked-in tasting room, an Islington crowd that knows its skin-contact. Quietly excellent.

hook: Two-floor organic and biodynamic wine bar off Carnaby Street
facts: On Newburgh Street in the Carnaby quarter for over fifteen years
Pours only organic and biodynamic wine from small artisan growers
Spread over two floors with a small outdoor terrace
French-leaning kitchen with a weekly-changing menu
w: A two-floor hold-out on Newburgh Street, behind the Carnaby foot traffic, pouring only organic and biodynamic bottles for over fifteen years. Downstairs a bar for a quick glass, upstairs a kitchen of French plates that shift weekly. The terrace seats a lucky few. Soho natural wine before the hashtag found it.

hook: Laid-back natural wine bar with craft beer and a vinyl habit
facts: On Drayton Park, a short walk from Arsenal's stadium
Stocks French, Spanish, Italian and English low-intervention wine
Ran as a bottle shop through lockdown, then reopened the bar
Hosts quiz nights, tastings and a book club
w: Not a library, despite the name, but a snug Drayton Park bar that runs on natural wine, craft beer and a turntable. Through lockdown it survived as a bottle shop; the by-the-glass counter is back upstairs. Records spin, dogs get water and treats, the quiz fills the room. A proper neighbourhood local that happens to drink well.

hook: Deli of natural wine and raw-milk cheese that flips into a tapas bar
facts: Original site on Holloway Road, trading since 2015
Stocks over 100 cheeses and 120 wines, heavy on natural growers
Deli and shop by day, becomes a pintxos bar called TOPA by night
Second branch on Hackney Road
w: By day a Holloway Road deli stacked with raw-milk cheese and natural wine, over a hundred of each; by night the lights drop and it turns into TOPA, a pintxos bar elbow-to-elbow with regulars. Europe's small producers under one roof, sold to take home or drink on the spot. The neighbourhood gem, relaunched.

hook: Britain's largest urban winery, a working cellar you drink inside
facts: Opened December 2025 in the Dock Shed at Canada Water
A 6,000 sq ft floor with a fully working 100-tonne winery
Over 100 wines by the glass from self-serve tap-and-pour machines
Grapes sourced from within two hours of London
w: The biggest urban winery in Britain, opened late 2025 in Canada Water's Dock Shed: a hundred-tonne working cellar you can stand inside, tasting straight from tank or barrel. Grapes trucked in from within two hours of London, then more than a hundred wines on self-pour taps. Wine-making as spectator sport, by the glass.
